title: Employer-Specific Applications, Techniques, and Safety
narrationScript: Two shops can work to the same code and still run completely different visual testing programs. One inspects machined aerospace fittings under a bench magnifier in a clean, climate controlled room. The other sends you across a tank floor on your knees with a flashlight and a pit gauge. The code sets the floor. Your employer's documents build the actual house. This lesson is about working inside that house. The applications, the techniques, the lighting, the materials, the special acceptance criteria, and the safety rules that keep you healthy while you do it. Start with the paperwork. Customer requirements flow down through the purchase order into your employer's quality system, and they land on your bench as a procedure and a job specification. Before you examine anything, confirm three things. You are holding the revision called out on the job traveler, not the copy from your last job. The procedure invokes the right code and edition. And every piece of measuring equipment, including your light meter, is in calibration. Employer specifications also add things codes never mention. Hold points where the customer witnesses your examination. Photographic documentation requirements. Cosmetic standards for surfaces the public will see. Now applications and techniques. In a fabrication shop, visual testing happens three times. Before welding, you check fit up, root opening, bevel angle, and cleanliness. During welding, you check interpass condition and back gouged surfaces, your last look at metal that is about to be buried. After welding, you do the final examination against the acceptance table. In service, the targets change. You are looking for corrosion, distortion, mechanical damage, and cracking at stress risers. Access drives technique. If you can get your eye within six hundred millimeters of the surface at thirty degrees or better, that is direct visual testing, helped by mirrors and magnifiers. If you cannot, you move to remote methods, and your procedure must show that the remote system resolves detail at least as well as your eye would. Lighting is a technique, not an accident. Most procedures require at least five hundred lux for general examination and one thousand lux for critical examination, measured with a calibrated meter at the surface you are examining. Not at your eye, and not at the lamp. But the number is only the start. Direction matters as much as intensity. Light raking across a surface at a low angle, five to thirty degrees, throws shadows from the smallest ridge or depression, and that is how you make a shallow dent, a tight crack, or fine porosity jump out. Light aimed straight down flattens everything. On shiny surfaces the problem reverses. A bare bulb puts a hot spot of glare exactly where you want to look. Diffuse the light, or move your eye away from the mirror angle, the angle where the reflection bounces straight back at you. Carry one good flashlight and practice sweeping it across welds at different angles. You will find more with angle changes than with brightness changes. Materials change what you look for and how you treat the part. Carbon steel hides fine detail under mill scale and rust, so surface preparation rules matter. Austenitic stainless steel is sensitive to chlorides, which drive pitting and stress corrosion cracking. That is why shops require low chloride markers and cleaners, and dedicated stainless brushes. Titanium tells on its welder. Straw, blue, or grey color along a weld means the metal saw air while it was hot. Titanium also must never see chlorinated solvents. Aluminum is soft, and smeared metal can fold over and hide a crack. Composites show impact damage, crushed core, and delamination edges, and the wrong solvent attacks the resin. Many employers add special evaluation criteria on top of the code. Tighter undercut limits. Zero arc strikes. Comparator photographs that define how much spatter is too much. When the customer's invoked specification is tighter than the code, the tighter requirement governs your evaluation. Finally, safety. Visual testing puts you close to the work, and close means exposed. Confined spaces, tanks, vessels, and pits require a permit, a tested atmosphere, isolation and lockout, an attendant, and lighting that is safe for the space. Low voltage, or rated for flammable atmospheres. Never look at a welding arc with unprotected eyes. Watch for hot surfaces, falls, and suspended loads. And treat chemicals with respect. Read the safety data sheet before you open a solvent can. Ventilate. Wear the right gloves. Remember that contamination control protects the hardware just as the safety rules protect you. The inspector who knows the employer's documents and respects the hazards is the one who gets the difficult, interesting jobs.

sections.heading: Flow-Down: From Customer Spec to Your Bench
sections.body:
Codes set minimums; employers build programs. Customer requirements enter through the contract and purchase order, flow into the employer's quality manual and **written practice** (the SNT-TC-1A document defining how examiners are trained, examined, and certified), and reach you as a **VT procedure** plus a job-specific traveler or inspection plan. Employer specifications routinely add what codes never address: customer hold and witness points, photographic documentation requirements, marking restrictions, cosmetic-surface standards, and sampling plans.

Three checks before any examination. First, *revision control*: work only to the revision listed on the traveler — an outdated procedure is an automatic nonconformance even if your findings are correct. Second, *code alignment*: the procedure must invoke the governing code and edition named in the contract. Third, *equipment status*: light meter, magnifiers of the stated power, weld gauges — all within calibration, with stickers or records to prove it. Some procedures also require a documented resolution demonstration, such as confirming you can resolve a fine line of specified width on the surface under the actual job lighting before starting. Treat these checks as part of the examination, not paperwork around it; auditors certainly do.

sections.caption: An employer VT procedure follows a predictable anatomy — and four of its sections deserve verification before the first part is examined.

sections.heading: Applications and Techniques
sections.body:
Where VT happens dictates how it is done. **Fabrication:** fit-up examination (root opening, bevel angle, alignment, cleanliness) before the first arc; in-process checks of interpass cleaning and back-gouged surfaces — your last look at metal that will be buried; final examination of the completed weld against the acceptance table. **In-service:** corrosion surveys on piping and tank floors, distortion checks, and cracking at stress risers — nozzles, attachment welds, threads — typically under API-type in-service rules. **Manufacturing:** castings for cold shuts, shrinkage, and sand inclusions; machined parts for tears, smears, and handling damage; aerospace assemblies for fastener condition and foreign object debris.

Access selects the technique. Direct VT — eye within 600 mm (24 in.) of the surface, at not less than 30° — comes first, assisted by inspection mirrors and a 5× to 10× magnifier where the procedure permits. Where the eye cannot go, remote VT (rigid borescopes, fiberscopes, video probes) takes over, and the procedure must show the remote system's resolution is at least equivalent to direct viewing. Choose tools deliberately: a mirror plus a good flashlight often beats a borescope on setup time and image quality whenever any line of sight exists.

sections.caption: Materials commonly examined by VT, what to look for on each, and the handling rules that protect them.
rows: Carbon steel weldments
Undercut, porosity, spatter, arc strikes, crater cracks
Remove scale and rust per procedure before evaluating fine detail
Austenitic stainless steel
Weld defects, heat tint, pitting, chloride stress-corrosion cracking in service
Low-chloride / low-halide markers and cleaners; dedicated stainless brushes only
Titanium
Weld discoloration (straw / blue / grey signals contamination), cracks
No chlorinated solvents; halogen-free markers only
Aluminum
Soft-metal smearing that masks cracks, handling damage
Gentle preparation; avoid aggressive abrasives that fold metal over flaws
Castings
Cold shuts, shrinkage, sand inclusions, surface porosity
Surface texture sets detectability; evaluate against comparator plates or photos
Composites
Impact damage, delamination edges, crushed core, resin starvation
Approved cleaners only — the wrong solvent attacks the resin
Coated or painted surfaces
Blisters, holidays, rust bleed-through, mechanical damage
Do not strip coatings unless the procedure directs it

sections.heading: Lighting Techniques
sections.body:
Meet the number first: typical procedure minimums are about 500 lux (50 fc) for general examination and 1000 lux (100 fc) for critical examination, verified with a **calibrated light meter with the sensor at the examination surface** — not at your eye, not at the lamp. Record the reading when the procedure requires it.

Then use direction, because geometry finds flaws that brightness alone never will:

- **Raking (oblique) light**, about 5–30° to the surface, casts shadows from shallow relief — dents, weld toes, tight cracks, fine porosity.
- **Near-perpendicular light** flattens relief but is best for judging color: heat tint, corrosion products, coating condition.
- **Diffuse light** (bounced, or passed through a diffuser) controls glare on polished or machined surfaces.
- **Off-specular viewing**: glare is a mirror-angle problem — move your eye or the lamp so the specular reflection misses you.
- **Backlighting** reveals through-holes and, on translucent materials, internal condition.

A single bright flashlight swept across a weld at changing angles is the cheapest crack-finding instrument in the kit — make the sweep a habit, not an afterthought.

sections.caption: Raking light throws shadows from shallow relief, while glare control means diffusing the source or keeping the eye off the specular angle — and the meter reading is always taken at the surface.

sections.heading: Special Evaluation Criteria
sections.body:
Many employers and customers evaluate to criteria tighter than the code default, and the contract makes those criteria binding. Expect to see: tightened undercut or reinforcement limits on fatigue-loaded joints; **zero tolerance for arc strikes** outside the weld groove; spatter limits defined by comparator photographs; cosmetic-grade surface requirements on visible architectural or consumer product; cleanliness standards verified visually before coating or assembly; and sampling plans that escalate to 100% examination when a reject appears.

Two rules keep you out of trouble. First, *the invoked document governs*: when a customer specification flowed down by the purchase order is more stringent than the code, you evaluate to the customer specification — the contract's order-of-precedence language settles any conflict, and open questions go to your Level III, not to a guess. Second, *special criteria must be written*: a verbal 'the customer wants it cleaner than that' is not an acceptance basis. If a requirement matters, it appears in a controlled document with a revision, and your report cites exactly which document and paragraph you evaluated against.

sections.heading: Safety Rules for VT Operations
sections.body:
VT puts your face close to the hardware, which puts you close to the hazards. Non-negotiables:

- **Confined spaces** (vessels, tanks, pits): entry permit, tested atmosphere — oxygen, flammables, toxics — isolation and lockout/tagout of connected lines and rotating equipment, an attendant, and a rescue plan before anyone's head crosses the plane of the opening.
- **Lighting in confined or wet spaces**: low-voltage or appropriately protected equipment; in potentially flammable atmospheres, only equipment rated for the classified area.
- **Welding areas**: never view an arc with unprotected eyes — flash burn is a real inspection injury; shield against UV, sparks, and hot surfaces during in-process VT.
- **Working at height and around lifts**: fall protection where required; never position yourself under a suspended load to get a better view.
- **Operating plants**: respect permits-to-work, hot-work controls, and radiation boundaries posted by nearby radiography crews.

A finding is never urgent enough to bypass a permit. The examination waits; it always waits.

sections.title: Chemical contamination cuts both ways
sections.body: Solvents and cleaners can hurt you and the hardware. Protect yourself: read the SDS before opening the can, ventilate, wear the gloves the SDS calls for, and keep flammables away from ignition sources. Protect the part: chlorinated solvents and high-chloride residues promote stress-corrosion cracking on austenitic stainless and nickel alloys and are forbidden on titanium; use only approved low-chloride / low-halogen cleaners and markers, and only employer-approved chemicals on composites. When in doubt, the approved-materials list in your procedure — not the nearest spray can — decides.

figures.title: Anatomy of an Employer VT Procedure
Lighting Technique: Direction Finds What Brightness Misses

checkQuestions.question: Your procedure requires 1000 lux at the examination surface. The correct way to verify this is:
options: A calibrated light meter reading taken with the sensor at the surface being examined
A meter reading taken at your eye position
The lamp manufacturer's lumen rating from the box
A smartphone app reading taken anywhere in the room
checkQuestions.explanation: Illuminance requirements apply at the examination surface, so the calibrated sensor goes where the examination happens — readings at the eye or ratings on the lamp prove nothing about the surface.

checkQuestions.question: To make a shallow dent or a tight surface crack stand out, the most effective lighting technique is:
options: Bright light aimed perpendicular to the surface
Low-angle raking light that casts shadows across the surface relief
Diffuse overhead room lighting only
Reducing total illumination below 200 lux
checkQuestions.explanation: Light raking across the surface at roughly 5–30° throws shadows from even shallow relief; perpendicular light flattens relief and hides exactly these flaws.

checkQuestions.question: Which marking and cleaning restriction is typical when inspecting austenitic stainless steel?
options: Only graphite pencils may be used on the surface
Use low-chloride / low-halide markers and cleaners, with dedicated stainless brushes
Any chlorinated solvent is acceptable as long as it evaporates
Markers of every kind are banned on all alloys
checkQuestions.explanation: Chlorides and other halides promote pitting and stress-corrosion cracking in austenitic stainless, so shops control them in markers, cleaners, and tools that touch the surface.

checkQuestions.question: A customer specification invoked by the purchase order sets a tighter undercut limit than the code default. You should evaluate to:
options: The code default, because codes outrank customer documents
The tighter customer limit invoked by the contract and your procedure
The average of the two values
Whichever value the welder agrees with
checkQuestions.explanation: Flowed-down special evaluation criteria are binding; when an invoked specification is more stringent than the code, the tighter requirement governs the evaluation.

checkQuestions.question: Before performing VT inside a vessel that previously held flammable product, you must:
options: Ventilate it briefly and proceed if it smells clear
Confirm a confined-space permit, tested atmosphere, isolation and lockout, an attendant, and lighting rated for the space
Enter quickly to limit your exposure time
Bring a second flashlight as a backup
checkQuestions.explanation: Confined-space entry requires permit, atmosphere testing, isolation/LOTO, an attendant, and suitably rated equipment — no finding justifies bypassing the permit process.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level II §10.1–10.6 and §6.4 (chemicals contamination)
SNT-TC-1A employer written practice framework (generic reference)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 illumination and direct viewing practice (generic reference)
Confined-space entry and lockout/tagout regulatory practice, OSHA-type rules (generic reference)
Employer procedure and customer specification flow-down practice (generic reference)
